title: Build a Content System
tagline: Not one blog post. A machine. Produce great content in your voice, consistently, in a fraction of the time.
outcome: When you finish this project you will have a complete content system: a Claude Project with your voice dialed in, a set of reusable templates, a four-stage production workflow, and a monthly feedback loop. You will produce a week of content in about twenty minutes, in a voice that sounds like you.
submission_prompt: Paste a link to one piece of content you produced with your new system (a LinkedIn post, a newsletter, a blog post). Optionally add a one-sentence note on how long it took compared to before.

steps.title: Define the foundation
steps.body:
Before you build anything, write down four things: **audience**, **platform**, **topics**, and **goal**.

- **Audience:** who reads it, what they care about, and (just as important) what they are tired of hearing.
- **Platform:** where you publish and how often.
- **Topics:** the three or four things you cover, specifically.
- **Goal:** why you create content in the first place. Inbound leads? Authority? Community? A job offer?

Specific beats generic every time. *Mid-level PMs and Series A-B founders* beats *people in tech*. *Product-led growth and user research that changes roadmaps* beats *startup advice*.

The foundation shapes every decision the system makes. If it is vague here, the output will be vague everywhere.

**Why specificity in audience matters more than anything else.** Every element of a content system — the topics you pick, the hooks you write, the examples you choose, the tone you use — should flow from a crystal-clear picture of who you are writing for. Vague audience definitions produce content that tries to speak to everyone and resonates with no one. Specific audience definitions produce content that makes your actual reader feel like you wrote it for them personally. That feeling is what drives shares, follows, and replies.

**How to get specific about audience.** Think of one real person who represents your ideal reader. Not a demographic — a person. What do they do on Monday morning? What is the problem they are trying to solve at work this week? What have they read five times before that they are bored of? What would make them stop scrolling? Write three sentences about that person. That is your audience definition. Every piece of content you produce should be written for that person.

**How to define topics that are genuinely yours.** Your topics should sit at the intersection of three things: what you know deeply, what your audience needs, and what you are not seeing covered well by other creators in your space. The last one is the differentiator. Topics that are well-covered by others already require you to be demonstrably better to win attention. Topics where you have unique perspective or access and others have not gone deep — those are where you build real authority fastest.

**The foundation prompt.** Open Claude and paste: *I am building a content system. Help me pressure-test my foundation. Audience: [your definition]. Platform: [where and how often]. Topics: [your list]. Goal: [your goal]. Tell me: (1) Is my audience specific enough to generate targeted content, or do I need to narrow it? (2) Are my topics differentiated enough to stand out, or are they generic? (3) Is my goal concrete enough to measure, or do I need to define success more precisely?* Use Claude's feedback to sharpen each element before moving to setup.
steps.done_when: You have written down your audience, platform, topics, and goal with enough specificity that a stranger could predict what you would post about.

steps.title: Set up your Claude Project
steps.body:
Create a **Project** in Claude. Name it clearly. This is where your content system lives.

Write custom instructions in four sections:

1. **About Me** — role, experience, your unique angle.
2. **My Audience** — the definition you wrote in step 1.
3. **My Voice** — how you sound. This is the section that matters most.
4. **Rules** — platform constraints (word count, hashtag limits, banned phrases).

For the voice section, replace adjectives with specifics. *Professional and authoritative* is a generic default. *Like explaining to a smart colleague over coffee. Short paragraphs. Open with an observation from real experience. Never say leverage, synergy, or at the end of the day* is a voice.

Upload 3-5 examples of your best content into the Project. **Voice examples beat voice descriptions.** Claude learns more from five real posts than from a paragraph of adjectives. Start with posts that performed well and sound the most like you.

**How to write the About Me section.** This is not a bio. It is context Claude needs to write authentically from your perspective. Include: your role and industry, how long you have been doing it, what you have seen and built that most people in your space have not, and your specific angle or point of view. The angle is the most important part. *VP of Sales at a Series B SaaS company who previously ran a 12-person SDR team and before that was a rep for three years* gives Claude much more useful context than *sales professional.*

**How to write the Voice section.** Start by describing what you do NOT sound like. Ruling out common defaults is faster than describing a positive. *Not corporate. Not listicle-style. Not self-promotional. Not abstract thought leadership.* Then add what you do sound like: *Specific. First-person observations from real situations. One core idea per piece. Short sentences when making the point. Longer sentences when adding texture. Humor is dry, not self-deprecating.* Then add the banned phrases: the words and constructions that you never use because they sound like everyone else.

**How to write the Rules section.** For LinkedIn: *Maximum 1,300 characters for posts. No more than three hashtags. Never start with "I" as the first word. Every post ends with a question or a provocative statement, never a call to follow.* For a newsletter: *Maximum 600 words. Three sections max. Each section has a one-line header. Plain text, no bold mid-sentence. CTA is always one sentence at the end, never a button graphic.* For a blog: *1,000-1,500 words. Five to seven H2 headers. No H3s. Conclusion is a single sentence that echoes the opening.* Encode whatever constraints your platform and style require.

**Choosing which examples to upload.** Upload the pieces that are most authentically yours — not the ones that got the most engagement if those required you to write in a style you do not normally use. The goal is to teach Claude your baseline voice, not your highest-performing outlier voice. Three to five is the right number. Fewer and the voice sample is too thin. More and Claude averages them together rather than internalizing the patterns.
steps.done_when: Your Project exists with all four custom-instruction sections filled out and 3-5 example pieces uploaded.

steps.title: Calibrate the voice
steps.body:
Before you generate anything, run the **voice verification test.** Ask Claude: *Based on my instructions and the examples I uploaded, describe my content voice back to me.*

Read the response carefully. If it matches how you see yourself, you nailed it. If it does not, adjust. If Claude says *professional and authoritative* and you actually write casually with humor, your instructions are too generic. Add specifics. Add examples. Test again.

This loop is the single highest-leverage step in the whole setup. If Claude cannot describe your voice correctly, it cannot write in your voice. Do not skip to generating content until the description comes back accurate.

Two or three calibration passes is normal. Each pass takes two minutes and prevents hours of editing later.

**The exact calibration prompt.** Paste this into your Project: *Based on my custom instructions and the examples I uploaded, describe my content voice back to me in detail. Cover: (1) tone — how I sound to a reader, (2) sentence structure — how I construct sentences and paragraphs, (3) the specific words and phrases I use repeatedly, (4) the specific words and phrases I never use, (5) how I open pieces, (6) how I close pieces, (7) what makes my voice distinct from a generic professional in my field.* Read each point of the response and check it against your own sense of how you write.

**What to do when the description is wrong.** Identify the specific mismatch. If Claude says you use formal language and you write conversationally, the problem is either in the instructions (too formal a description) or in the examples (your uploaded examples are your more formal pieces). Fix the root cause. If the instructions say *professional* where they should say *direct and casual*, update the instruction. If the examples are your most polished pieces rather than your most authentic ones, swap them out. Then run the calibration prompt again.

**The common calibration failure modes.** Generic adjectives in the instructions (professional, authoritative, engaging) produce generic voice descriptions back. Fix: replace every adjective with a specific construction or example. Inconsistent uploaded examples (a mix of casual posts and formal articles) produce averaged voice descriptions that do not match either. Fix: pick examples from one mode. Absent banned-phrase lists produce output that uses the phrases you hate most. Fix: add five specific banned phrases to the rules section.

**When to stop calibrating.** You have calibrated enough when you read Claude's voice description and find yourself nodding rather than correcting. You do not need the description to be perfect — you need it to be accurate enough that when Claude generates content, your editing burden drops to style-matching rather than full rewrites. One test: take the best sentence from Claude's voice description and imagine seeing it in your bio. If you would keep it as-is, you are calibrated.
steps.done_when: Claude's description of your voice matches how you actually write well enough that you would use its words to describe yourself.

steps.title: Build reusable templates
steps.body:
A template is a reusable prompt that produces consistent output. Build at least one per content type you publish.

Every good template has five pieces: **content type**, **structure** (hook / body / CTA or whatever your format is), **input variables** (what changes each time), **quality criteria per section**, and **output format**.

Quality criteria are where templates get sharp. *Write a good hook* produces generic hooks. *Hook must be a bold observation or counterintuitive take that stops the scroll. Not a question, not a statistic. Ideally under 12 words* produces scroll-stopping hooks.

Input variables show Claude what you will provide each time: *Topic, Key Insight, Specific Example*. Everything else stays constant.

**Templates evolve.** If the hook always needs editing, the hook instructions are not specific enough. If the CTA feels forced, you need better CTA examples. Update templates based on what needs editing. They get sharper over time.

**The LinkedIn post template structure.** Use this as a starting point and customize each section to your voice: *Content type: LinkedIn post. Structure: Hook (1-2 lines) → Context or story (2-3 short paragraphs) → Insight or takeaway (1 paragraph) → Engagement question or statement (1 line). Quality criteria — Hook: bold observation, under 15 words, no questions, no statistics. Context: first-person, one specific situation, detail that makes it feel real. Insight: one clear idea, no hedging, no "it depends." Closing: invites response without asking for follows. Input variables this session: Topic = [X], Key Insight = [Y], Specific Example = [Z]. Output format: plain text, line breaks between paragraphs, no hashtags until I ask.*

**How to write quality criteria that actually constrain.** For each section, write the criteria as a combination of positive requirements and negative prohibitions. Hook example: *Must be an observation or counterintuitive claim (positive). Must NOT be a question, a statistic, or a list opener (negative). Must be under 15 words (constraint). Must create a reason to keep reading without overpromising (purpose).* Four elements — what it must do, what it must not do, any quantitative constraint, and why it exists. Templates with all four elements produce dramatically more consistent output than templates with only the positive requirement.

**Building a template library.** You will eventually want one template per format: one for LinkedIn posts, one for newsletter issues, one for Twitter/X threads, one for short blog posts. Build the first one completely and test it through several pieces before building the next. A well-tested single template is more valuable than four draft templates. Once the first one is working, the second takes half as long because you already know what quality criteria matter.

**Where to save templates.** Paste the final template into the Project description field in Claude so it is always one click away. Also copy it to a note or doc with the name *[Platform] Template — v[number] — [Date]*. Version the templates so you know which is current and can roll back if a new version underperforms.
steps.done_when: You have at least one reusable template saved (in the Project description, a note, or a separate doc) with structure, variables, and quality criteria per section.

steps.title: Run the four-stage production workflow
steps.body:
Four stages: **Ideate** → **Draft** → **Edit** → **Polish.**

1. **Ideate.** *Give me 10 angles on [topic] aimed at [audience].* Pick the strongest. Personal and specific beats abstract and thought-leadership every time.
2. **Draft.** Run the chosen angle through your template. First draft lands in 30 seconds.
3. **Edit.** This is the stage that separates good from great. Ask Claude: *Review this against my voice guidelines. Check: hook grabs attention, specific example present, sounds like me, right length, closing prompts engagement. Give me the edited version and list what you changed.* Seeing what Claude changed teaches you what to watch for.
4. **Polish.** Platform formatting: line breaks, hashtag count, length check, final read-through.

**Batch production is the power move.** Run three topics through the whole workflow in one session. Claude has your full context loaded. Voice stays consistent across pieces. Twenty minutes for a week of content.

**How to run the Ideate stage well.** The ideate prompt that works: *Give me 10 angles on [topic] for [audience description]. Each angle should be: (1) specific enough to make one clear point, (2) based on an observation or experience rather than an abstract claim, (3) different from the others — no two angles about the same sub-idea. Score each one 1-10 on how likely it is to resonate with [audience] and explain your reasoning.* Review the scored list. The highest-scoring angle is usually right, but sometimes the second or third is the one that feels more authentically yours. Pick the one you would most want to read, not the one you think you should post.

**How to run the Draft stage.** Copy the angle you picked. Paste your template. Fill in the input variables: *Topic: [angle]. Key Insight: [what you want readers to take away]. Specific Example: [a real situation from your experience that illustrates it].* The specific example is the most important variable. Generic examples produce generic drafts. A real situation with a real detail — a specific meeting, a specific number, a specific name-redacted client situation — produces a draft that sounds like you.

**How to run the Edit stage.** The edit prompt: *Review this draft against my voice guidelines and the template quality criteria. Check each section: (1) hook — does it meet the criteria? (2) context — does it include a specific example? (3) insight — is it one clear idea? (4) closing — does it invite engagement without asking for follows? Produce the edited version and list every change you made with a one-line explanation of why.* Reading what Claude changed is a learning experience. Over time you will see the same patterns — the same types of additions and removals — and you will start catching them yourself in the draft stage.

**The batch production workflow.** On your production day, prepare your inputs first: three topics, the key insight for each, and one specific example for each. Then run all three through Ideate → Draft → Edit in sequence without leaving the Project. The first piece calibrates Claude's context. The second and third benefit from that calibration. All three should sound like the same person wrote them — because you designed the system to guarantee that.
steps.done_when: You have run the full workflow end to end on at least one real piece you will actually publish.

steps.title: Schedule the feedback loop
steps.body:
A static content system is a good system. A self-improving one is a great system.

Once a month, feed Claude your recent posts with their metrics: *Here are my last N posts and how they performed. What patterns separate the winners from the losers? Look at hook style, topic type, length, and CTA format.*

Then encode the patterns back into the system. If winners always open with a personal anecdote and a specific number, update the hook instruction to require that. If generic questions consistently flop, ban them in the CTA section.

**Validate before you publish.** Generate 3 test posts with the updated template and compare them side-by-side with your old output. If the changes land, keep them. If not, revert.

Put the review on your calendar: first Monday of every month, 30 minutes. That is the whole maintenance cost.

**The monthly review prompt.** On the first Monday of each month, paste your last month of posts with their engagement numbers into the Project and run: *Here are my posts from the last month with engagement metrics. Analyze the patterns that separate high-performing from low-performing posts. Look at: hook style (observation vs question vs statistic vs story opener), topic type (tactical vs strategic vs personal vs industry commentary), length (short vs medium), and closing format (question vs statement vs CTA). For each pattern you find, tell me: (1) what the winning version looks like, (2) what the losing version looks like, and (3) the specific instruction change I should make to my template to encode the winning pattern.*

**How to encode patterns back into the system.** Take each instruction change Claude suggests and update three places: the custom instructions in the Project, the template, and your prompt changelog. The changelog entry should read: *[Date]: Updated hook instruction based on 4-week performance data. Winning hooks were all first-person observations with a specific number or situation. Updated: "Hook must be a first-person observation that includes a specific detail (number, situation, or before/after)." Removed: "Bold observation or counterintuitive take."*

**How to validate changes before committing.** Generate three posts with the updated template on topics where you already know what a good post looks like. Compare the new output to the old output on the same topics. Is the new output better on the dimension you were targeting? Is it worse on any dimension you were not trying to change? If the update improves hook quality but makes the body less specific, the update has a side effect you need to fix before committing.

**The compounding effect.** A system that improves by even 10% per month doubles its output quality in roughly eight months. A system that stays static is exactly as good in month eight as it was in month one. The feedback loop is what separates a content system from a content template. Templates are static. Systems learn. The thirty minutes you spend on the first Monday of each month is the difference between the two.
steps.done_when: You have a calendar reminder (or a note somewhere you will see it) scheduling your first monthly feedback-loop review.

steps.title: Ship it
steps.body:
Publish the piece you produced in step 5. Real content. Not a draft. Out in the world.

Grab the link and paste it into the submission form below. If you are optimizing for speed, include a note on how long it took compared to your old process.

You did not just learn to use AI for writing. You built **content infrastructure.** Voice is baked in. Structure is consistent. Quality is checked. Production is batched. The system gets sharper every month through the feedback loop.

You are a content system designer now.

**What you actually built.** Most people who use AI for content treat it like a faster keyboard — they type a topic, they get a post, they edit it, they post it. You built something different. You built a system with institutional memory (the Project), a quality standard (the calibrated voice and templates), a production process (the four-stage workflow), and a self-improvement loop (the monthly review). Those four elements together are what separate content creators who burn out from content creators who compound.

**The leverage you unlocked.** Time leverage: you produce in twenty minutes what used to take two hours. Quality leverage: every piece goes through an edit stage that catches the common failures before they reach your audience. Consistency leverage: your voice sounds the same across pieces because it is encoded in the system, not dependent on what mood you are in when you write. Compound leverage: the system improves itself monthly rather than staying static.

**What to build next.** Once you have run this system for four weeks, you will have enough performance data to run your first feedback-loop review. That review will sharpen the templates. After eight weeks, you will have a template that outperforms anything you could write from scratch on a random Tuesday. After twelve weeks, you will have a rhythm. Rhythm is the real goal — not any single piece, but the habit of consistent production that compounds into real audience growth.

**The prompt to share with a teammate.** If someone on your team creates content and you want to help them: *I built a content system using Claude Projects. Here is the setup: [paste your custom instructions]. Here is the template: [paste your template]. Here is the four-stage workflow: Ideate, Draft, Edit, Polish. It takes about twenty minutes to produce a week of content. Want me to walk you through setting up your own version?* Sharing the system is faster than explaining it. The system is the documentation.
steps.done_when: Your content is live at a public URL and you are ready to paste the link.
